_London/New York_ \| The Strait of Hormuz oil shock has yet to crash demand as the rich world borrows from its stocks and pays up to secure supply, but traders are now sounding the alarm that a harsh adjustment is coming.

The longer the vital oil channel does not reopen, traders said, the more consumption is going to have to recalibrate lower to align with supply that’s dropped at least 10 per cent.

And for that to happen, people will have to buy less, either through prices they cannot afford or government intervention to force consumption down.

![](https://static.ffx.io/images/$zoom_0.136%2C$multiply_4%2C$ratio_1.5%2C$width_756%2C$x_0%2C$y_0/t_crop_custom/c_scale%2Cw_620%2Cq_88%2Cf_auto/b3c34492247774736edc5969219de7f896022401)

Prolonged fuel inflation is starting to affect consumer behaviour, even in the US.  Max Mason-Hubers

A billion barrels of supply loss is already all but guaranteed – more than double the emergency inventories that governments released not long after the conflict began at the end of February.

[Buffers are being used up fast](https://www.afr.com/companies/energy/g7-to-discuss-joint-release-of-emergency-oil-reserves-20260309-p5o8v9), helping to keep a lid on oil prices for now. But with the closure of the strait now in its ninth week, demand destruction that started in less obvious sectors such as petrochemicals in Asia is quietly spreading to everyday markets all over the world.

Advertisement

“Demand destruction is happening in places that are not visible pricing centres,” Saad Rahim, chief economist of trading group Trafigura, told the _Financial Times’_ Commodities Global Summit in Lausanne last week.

“That adjustment is already happening, but if this continues, it has to get larger and larger. We’re at a critical inflection point.”

The most dependent industries and markets – including petrochemicals plants in Asia and the Middle East, and shipments of liquefied petroleum gas, a vital cooking fuel in India – suffered an immediate hit when the US and Israel first attacked Iran on February 28.

Now, with [a stalemate between US President Donald Trump and his Iranian adversaries](https://www.afr.com/world/middle-east/trump-scraps-us-trip-for-iran-talks-leaving-ceasefire-in-limbo-20260426-p5zr3r) dragging on, the impact is increasingly shifting west — and to products that are central to consumers’ everyday lives.

## Travel, consumption hit

Airlines in Europe and the US are cutting thousands of flights. Analysts are warning of weakness in consumption of petrol after prices hit $4 a gallon in the US, and diesel – used to power everything from trucks to construction equipment.

Advertisement

Global oil demand is on track to slump the most in five years this month, according to the International Energy Agency, which co-ordinated the emergency measures by major economies to counteract the supply shock.

Trading giant Gunvor Group estimates the loss could double next month to 5 million barrels a day, or 5 per cent of world supplies. The group, along with other major traders, sees a growing risk of economic recession. Other analysts and traders said the impact has already reached about the 4 million-a-day mark.

![](<Base64-Image-Removed>)

Airlines in Asia were among the first to react to the oil shock, with Vietnamese carriers and Air New Zealand cutting back routes. Getty

That toll is beginning to take shape. Germany has slashed economic growth forecasts in half, while the International Monetary Fund has trimmed global estimates, citing the war.

In the most “severe” of three scenarios modelled by the European Central Bank, Brent prices peak at $US145 a barrel and cut the region’s growth in half. Brent crude closed at about $US105 a barrel on Friday.

The need for oil demand and economic activity to adjust lower, most likely through prices that discourage consumption, will only increase with every day the strait stays shut.

Advertisement

## Coming in waves

Worldwide demand already faces a hit of 5.3 million barrels a day this quarter, and a 12-week disruption of Hormuz would propel dated Brent, the world’s key physical crude price, above this month’s record to $US154 a barrel, according to consultant FGE NexantECA.

“Because there is still no visible disaster” in the West, “people think everything is okay, and a bit higher pump prices are the only impact”, said Cuneyt Kazokoglu, FGE’s director of energy transition.

But demand destruction “will come and is coming in waves”.

“Asia was first in line, Africa is the next one. Europe has already started talking about the lack of some fuels and feeling the price impact.”

Ultimately, in a market where demand needs to adjust down to match lower supply, oil prices may be what drive that recalibration.

Advertisement

In extreme scenarios, where price alone forces the market to balance, FGE estimates that crude oil would need to surge to $US250 a barrel.

Several analysts said privately that extreme uncertainty about what will happen in the conflict makes it almost impossible to model the demand impact. But without a swift resolution, the economic consequences could be profound.

“If you don’t get any reopening in three months’ time, then the case becomes a macro issue where the world is about to fall into recession,” Frederic Lasserre, Gunvor’s head of research, told the _FT’s_ Commodities Global Summit in Lausanne. The firm has even stress-tested the prospect of oil spiking to $US200 or even $US300 a barrel.

Particularly sensitive areas are so-called middle distillates, which include diesel. Prices in Europe surpassed $US200 a barrel last month, the highest since 2022. In India, truck fleet operators are bracing for fuel rationing and the first significant diesel price increases in years.

“A few more weeks, we will start seeing announcements of problems with securing diesel supply – that’s the backbone of the world’s economy for moving goods around,” Vikas Dwivedi, a strategist at Macquarie Group, said in a _Bloomberg_ interview.

Advertisement

“When it hits diesel, that is when we will all know it and feel it.”

## Aviation particularly vulnerable

Aviation is also particularly vulnerable. Airlines in Asia were among the first to react, with Vietnamese carriers and Air New Zealand cutting back routes.

Now the impact is spreading, with Deutsche Lufthansa scrubbing 20,000 short-haul flights from its European summer schedule and KLM curbing operations.

Even in the US – relatively shielded from the crisis by its domestic energy abundance – United Airlines Holdings is reducing planned growth by about 5 per cent, and now expects capacity, or available seat miles, in the second half of 2026 to be flat to up about 2 per cent from a year earlier.

Petrol is starting to feel the effect. American drivers may be spending more on the fuel, but with average prices above $US4 they’re buying 5 per cent fewer gallons than a year ago, according to Barclays.

Advertisement

“Higher prices over the past month and a half have led to fuel demand destruction from the US consumer,” analysts at the bank said.

In the weeks after the war erupted, consuming nations moved to buy themselves some time.

IEA nations such as the US, Germany and Japan announced an unprecedented release of 400 million barrels to plug the yawning supply gap, and China also tapped its buffer. Yet depleting such inventories wears down the world’s safeguards, ultimately leaving it more exposed.

“We’ve borrowed supply,” said Russell Hardy, chief executive of Vitol Group, the largest independent trader. “But you can’t do that forever. There are recessionary consequences from having to ration that demand.”

_London_ \| As athletes and spectators marvel at, not one, but two men smashing the once-thinkable two-hour barrier in this year’s London marathon, German sportswear maker Adidas also has reason to celebrate.

Kenya’s Sabastian Sawe and Ethiopia’s Yomif Kejelcha were both clad in the brand’s new and ultra-light $US500 ($700) racing shoe as they crossed the finishing line on Sunday (Monday AEST).

![](https://cf-images.ap-southeast-2.prod.boltdns.net/v1/static/3910869709001/561a7460-3234-48ec-aed3-ad39a5b0da8a/3505782d-c79e-4d22-97dd-f1a77eefa098/1280x720/match/image.jpg)

Kenyan Sabastian Sawe becomes the first man in history to officially break the two-hour barrier in a marathon, smashing the world record in London.

Sawe pulled away from Kejelcha in the final leg of the race to achieve a stunning world-record time of 1 hour 59 minutes and 30 seconds, more than a minute faster than the previous best for the event.

Kejelcha finished just 11 seconds behind, a remarkable feat in his first-ever competition at the 42.2-kilometre distance.

After years of global obsession, lab-backed experiments and near-misses, the [marathon’s ultimate barrier](https://www.afr.com/world/europe/world-record-tumbles-in-london-marathon-thanks-to-super-shoe-20250428-p5lumq) finally fell as Sawe obliterated the world record previously held by the late Kelvin Kiptum, who set a time of 2:00:35 at the Chicago Marathon in October 2023.

Advertisement

Kejelcha stayed on Sawe’s heels over much of the course before fading down the final stretch, while Jacob Kiplimo of Uganda took bronze in 2:00.28, making for the most remarkable men’s marathon finish in history.

“What comes today is not for me alone,” Sawe said, “but for all of us today in London.”

![](https://static.ffx.io/images/$zoom_0.187%2C$multiply_4%2C$ratio_1.5%2C$width_756%2C$x_0%2C$y_0/t_crop_custom/c_scale%2Cw_620%2Cq_88%2Cf_auto/759fef1d226da8bdcb250dfd4cc8944557fe7df4)

Sabastian Sawe from Kenya crosses the finish line to win the London Marathon. AP

On a relatively flat London course on a mostly sunny day with a temperature of about 15 degrees – ideal for running – Sawe ran a faster second half, covering it in 59:01.

Coming in as the defending champion in London, Sawe said it was a “day to remember for me” and thanked the huge crowds who lined the streets of the British capital to witness one of the greatest performances in running.

In the women’s race, Ethiopia’s Tigst Assefa pulled away from Kenya’s Hellen Obiri in the last kilometre to win in a time of 2 hours 15 minutes and 41 seconds, slightly faster than last year, when she broke the women’s-only world record.

Advertisement

For Sawe, Kejelcha and Assefa, the common denominator was Adidas’ Adizero Adios Pro Evo 3, a shoe the company debuted last week and which weighs just 97 grams in a standard size. That’s the lightest-ever version of the sort of sneaker that has transformed the sport of distance running in the past decade.

“This is a testament to the years of hard work and dedication they have made, alongside our innovation team, who have built a super shoe which breaks new ground,” said Patrick Nava, general manager of running at Adidas.

![](<Base64-Image-Removed>)

Tigst Assefa celebrates with her Adidas shoe after winning the Women’s 2026 TCS London Marathon. Getty

[Since the release of the Nike Vaporfly](https://www.afr.com/companies/retail/how-nike-s-ceo-is-trying-to-win-back-the-sports-world-20260310-p5o940), the first supershoe, in 2017, the number of men and women breaking new time barriers has risen sharply.

Obiri was wearing On Holding’s LightSpray Cloudboom Strike, a $US330 laceless racing shoe that’s produced by robots. She smashed her own personal best in the process, taking second place in the women’s race behind Assefa.

Sawe even surpassed the time of Eliud Kipchoge, who in 2019 ran the distance in a time of 1:59.40 during a Nike-organised event in Vienna that was not eligible for world-record status. That event included a host of people pacing Kipchoge for most of the run.

Advertisement

In London, Sawe and Kejelcha pushed each other in the final kilometres of the race, long after leaving official pacers behind.

For brands, having a high-profile winner in their latest shoes adds a stamp of legitimacy. And running shoes are a booming business, with the US market expanding 13 per cent in the year through February to $US8.1 billion, according to market researcher Circana.

![](<Base64-Image-Removed>)

Sabastian Sawe and Yomif Kejelcha lead the pack in the London marathon.  Getty

It’s a major piece of the broader performance-shoe category, which may reach $US104 billion in global sales by 2030, according to data from Euromonitor.

Adidas has been rebuilding its running franchise in recent years, looking to capitalise on the booming popularity of a sport that’s helped support the rise of challenger brands including Hoka, On and Brooks.

The German company has benefited from strong demand for its comfy $US150 Adizero Evo SL sneaker, which is a less intense take on the marathon-racing shoe worn by Sawe and others.

The running push is part of chief executive Bjorn Gulden’s efforts to derive more profits from performance sports gear and reduce the brand’s dependence on fashion items like the Samba and Gazelle shoes.

_Washington_ \| US President Donald Trump says the armed man accused of storming a black-tie dinner full of politicians and journalists had written a manifesto ahead of his attack and had “a lot of hatred in his heart”.

Trump told Fox News that the suspect was “a sick guy” and that his family previously expressed concerns about him to law enforcement officials.

![](https://static.ffx.io/images/$zoom_0.189%2C$multiply_4%2C$ratio_1.5%2C$width_756%2C$x_0%2C$y_0/t_crop_custom/c_scale%2Cw_620%2Cq_88%2Cf_auto/60082bc06e2196b1bea7f88e5414bd458e28cfb0)

US first lady Melania Trump and President Donald Trump during the White House Correspondents’ Dinner. Bloomberg

An official named the suspect as Cole Tomas Allen, a 31-year-old from Torrance, California. He was arrested on Saturday night (Sunday AEST) at the Washington Hilton, where the White House Correspondents’ Association was holding its annual dinner, after running through a security screening area [armed with guns and knives](https://www.afr.com/world/north-america/trump-correspondents-dinner-evacuated-amid-security-alert-20260426-p5zr4f).

“When you read his manifesto, he hates Christians,” Trump said on Fox News’ _Sunday Briefing_ program.

The manifesto was sent to Allen’s family members shortly before the attack by the suspect, who called himself the “Friendly Federal Assassin”, a law enforcement official told _Reuters_.

Advertisement

The manifesto also appeared to criticise Trump administration policies, such as the immigration crackdown, the war in Iran and strikes on vessels in the Caribbean that the White House has said are engaged in the narcotics trade.

“Turning the other cheek is for when you yourself are oppressed. I’m not the person raped in a detention camp. I’m not the fisherman executed without trial,” Allen wrote in the document, according to a report in the _New York Post_, citing officials.

Flourish embed

In pictures: White House Correspondents’ Dinner shooting

In pictures: White House Correspondents’ Dinner shooting

![White House Deputy Chief of Staff Dan Scavino jumps over a chair in the immediate aftermath of the incident.](https://static.ffx.io/images/$zoom_0.153%2C$multiply_4%2C$ratio_1.5%2C$width_756%2C$x_0%2C$y_0/t_crop_custom/c_scale%2Cw_1240%2Cq_52%2Cf_auto/adc5135c5137b6755adbe663ea9e0b1456b00e9a)

White House Deputy Chief of Staff Dan Scavino jumps over a chair in the immediate aftermath of the incident. Getty Images

“I’m not a schoolkid blown up, or a child starved, or a teenage girl abused by the many criminals in this administration. Turning the other cheek when \*someone else\* is oppressed is not Christian behaviour; it is complicity in the oppressor’s crimes.”

The _Post_ also reported that Allen’s brother had notified a Connecticut police department about the manifesto.

Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche told NBC’s _Meet the Press_ that it was likely Allen wanted to target members of the Trump administration, “including the president”.

Advertisement

Blanche said the man had purchased the two firearms he was carrying “within the last couple of years”, and had been staying at the Washington Hilton, where the dinner has been held for a decade.

According to Reuters, the manifesto mocked the “insane” lack of security at the hotel where the event was held, the official said.

“Like, the one thing that I immediately noticed walking into the hotel is the sense of arrogance,” the author reportedly wrote. “I walk in with multiple weapons and not a single person there considers the possibility that I could be a threat.”

![](<Base64-Image-Removed>)

Cole Tomas Allen has been identified as the accused gunman arrested at the White House Correspondents’ dinner. Linkedin

Some attendees have criticised the security precautions as porous. Still, the assailant was taken down by agents before getting close to the ballroom, where there were about 2600 guests and some 700 staff.

Forty-five years ago, then-president Ronald Reagan was seriously wounded when he was shot on the sidewalk outside the same hotel, which is in the Dupont Circle neighbourhood about 2.4 kilometres from the White House.

Advertisement

Trump has used the security scare to push for approval and support for his plans to construct a lavish ballroom on the grounds of the White House.

“This event would never have happened with the Militarily Top Secret Ballroom currently under construction at the White House,” Trump wrote in a post on social media on Sunday. “It cannot be built fast enough!”

![](<Base64-Image-Removed>)

Agents stand guard after a shooter opened fire outside the ballroom. Getty

## Wave of panic

The ballroom at the Washington Hilton was packed with cabinet members and administration officials who were hustled out after a series of loud bangs outside the room sparked a wave of panic.

US Secret Service agents flooded the room to extract Trump, first lady Melania Trump, Vice President JD Vance, Secretary of State Marco Rubio, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent and Defence Secretary Pete Hegseth.

Advertisement

[Trump and those around him](https://www.afr.com/world/north-america/trump-s-media-dinner-date-was-expected-to-make-news-but-not-like-this-20260426-p5zr54) did not initially react to the gunshots, as they were deep in conversation with mentalist Oz Pearlman, who was due to provide the evening’s entertainment.

But shouts of “get down” caused some attendees to run towards the front stage, causing a commotion and catching the attention of Trump and others at the top table.

The suspect travelled by train from Los Angeles to Chicago and then to Washington, Blanche said.

He will be charged in federal court on Monday with assault of a federal officer, discharging a firearm and attempting to kill a federal officer, Blanche said.
